segments.text:
The science of vaccines
CLS-N390 Vaccines: Science and S o ciety

Learning Objectives
Incorporate all components of a vaccine to build one.
Describe what a vaccine is.
Compare and contrast the type of vaccines produced and under investigation.
Diagram the process used to evaulate vaccines for safety and efficacy.
01
03
02
04
Apply the scientific method to a given scenario.
05

What is a vaccine?
A biological product that can be used to safely induce an immune response that confers protection against infection and/or disease on subsequent exposure to a pathogen (Pollard & Bijker , 2021, p. 83).
Does not cause disease
Artificially mimics infection
Generates primary immune response = creation of memory B cells
How does a vaccine work?

How have vaccines helped?
Pollard, A. J., & Bijker , E. M. (2020). A guide to vaccinology: from basic principles to new developments. Nature Reviews Immunology , 1-18.
Types of Vaccines
Vaccine Categories

Live vaccines
No modification of virus or bacteria
Given small amount of infectious agent – mild disease occurs
No longer used
Example: smallpox
Modified to a weakened or inactivated state – remove part that is pathogenic
Widely used – elicits immune response
Examples: measles, mumps, rubella, chicken pox, influenza, oral polio….
Live active
Live attenuated

Non-living vaccines
Whole cell used – killed to render nonviable
Examples: whole-cell pertussis, influenza, polio, hepatitis A, rabies, 1896 - typhoid
Modification of toxin produced by bacteria
Anti-toxoid antibody production
Short lived ~10 years
Examples: tetanus, 1923 - diphtheria
Whole killed
Toxoid

Non-living vaccines
Purified protein, recombinant protein, polysaccharide, peptide
Only uses a portion of the pathogen to create an immune response
Examples: pertussis, influenza, hepatitis B, meningococcus, pneumococcus, hepatitis A, 1970 - anthrax
Created to look like the virus, but not be the virus
Examples: human papillomavirus, 1986 – hepatitis B
Subunit
Virus-like particle

Non-living vaccines
Gram negative bacteria outer cell  membrane with antigen attached
Used for Gram negative bacteria
Example: 1987 – Haemophilus influenzae type B
Polysaccharide chain used to elicit immune response – bound to carrier protein to make it travel better
Typically seen with bacteria
Example: Haemophilus influenzae type B, pneumococcal, typhoid,  1987 - meningococcal
Outer membrane vesicle
Protein-polysaccharide conjugate

Novel Vaccines
mRNA vaccines
Viral vector vaccines
Microneedle vaccines
The Scientific Method

The Scientific Method
Logical, problem-solving approach used by scientists
Typically starts with an observation that leads to a question and wanting to know more
Cancer biologist – might see that a certain kind of cancer cannot be treated with chemotherapy
Marine ecologist – might notice that a coral reef in the area is bleaching (turning white)
Steps to the Scientific Method
Make an observation
Form a question or identify a problem
Research – see if there are already answers out there
Form a hypothesis
Conduct experiment to test hypothesis
Draw conclusion from experiment data
Report interpretation
Why?

Scenario:
You are getting ready for an all-day study session about vaccines, and you need a good breakfast to get you started.
You are going to make a breakfast sandwich and coffee.
You put bread in the toaster, but it won’t toast.
The Scientific Method

You are a little frustrated because your eggs are now getting cold.
You grab your cup of coffee and ask…
The Scientific Method
Why?

You try to get the toaster to work and notice the light doesn’t come on and the coils do not get hot.
You make sure it is plugged in, and it is.
This makes you think…
The Scientific Method

To determine if your prediction that the outlet is broken is true, you need to come up with a hypothesis to test it.
A hypothesis isn’t always the right answer, but it is a way to help you determine the right answer.
The Scientific Method

You have made a hypothesis and now it is time to test it out.
Let's find another outlet and plug the toaster in.
The Scientific Method

Results of your test may support or contradict your hypothesis.
Support of hypothesis cannot conclusively provide it is correct but means that it is likely correct.
If results contradict a hypothesis, it is probably not true – unless there was a flaw in the test.
The Scientific Method
Vaccine Development

First step – observation of the need
Collaborative effort – academia, small biotech companies, large vaccine manufacturers, regulatory authorities/ government
Several obstacles to think about when creating a vaccine
Vaccine Development

Antigen
Blueprint that generates an immune response – must have!
Small portion of disease-causing organism – protein or polysaccharide
Toxin
DNA/RNA
Whole organism – weakened or attenuated

Preservatives
Prevent the vaccine from becoming contaminated once opened
Used for vaccines that have multiple doses – single dose vaccines do not have preservatives
Most common = 2-phenoxyethanol
Used for many years – safe and used in baby care products

Stabilizers
Prevent chemical reactions from occurring and keep vaccine components from sticking to vial
Regularly consumed for naturally produced by humans = no safety concern
Sugars (lactose, sucrose)
Amino acids (glycine)
Gelatin
Proteins (recombinant human albumin derived from yeast)

Surfactants
Reduce the surface tension of vaccine components
Acts as emulsifier = keeps ingredients blended together
Prevent settling and clumping
Used in many applications, such as ice cream

Diluent
Liquid used to dilute vaccine to correct concentration
Most common = sterile water

Residuals
Tiny amounts of various substance used during manufacturing – not active ingredients
Vary depending on manufacturing process – egg proteins, yeast, antibiotics
Measured in parts per million or parts per billion
Occasionally cause allergic reactions

Adjuvant
Improves immune response
Act by:
Keeping vaccine at injection site longer
Stimulating local immune cells
Providing way for vaccine to be absorbed
Most common = tiny amount of aluminum salts
Does not cause long-term health problems, regularly ingested through eating and drinking
Evaluating Vaccines for Efficacy and Safety

Pre-clinical Phase
Every vaccine begins at this phase. Vaccines are tested in an organ model to answer the following.
Is the vaccine safe? Adverse immune reactions are checked for in the established model organism.
What is a safe starting dose for humans? Multiple doses are tested to estimate the safest starting dose to use in humans during future phases.
Is this vaccine effective? This phase needs to show some efficacy in the animal model before it can ethically be tested in humans.
Typically, this phase takes approximately 1.5 years to complete and can cost an average of $20 million.
Success rate: 14% of vaccines make it thorough this phase.

Phase I
Phase I begins the initial trials in human subjects. Volunteers agree to be given the vaccine to test for safety and efficacy. Since this is the very first time the vaccine is given to people, we are talking about small test groups, only in the tens. In this phase, the following questions are answered.
Is the vaccine safe? Volunteers are checked for adverse immune system reactions, side effects, and evidence of toxicity.
What is the immune response in humans? The type of immune response and the extent of the immune response are determined in humans.
Typically, this phase takes several months to complete and can cost an average of $9 million.
Success rate: Approximately 32% of vaccines make it thorough this phase, which is only 4.5% of all vaccines that begin the clinical trial process.

Phase II
In this phase, volunteers are recruited from different age, gender, and ethnic backgrounds with various health statuses. Participants are placed in larger groups, typically in the hundreds. T he following questions are answered.
Is the vaccine still safe? The vaccine is continued to be assessed for safety and potential side effects are looked for in a more diverse study group.
How does the immune response differ among different people? The immune response is monitored, especially in regard to the subjects’
age, gender, and ethnicity. Additionally, the optimal dose is determined based on participant response to various doses.
What is the best way to administer the vaccine? The vaccine preparation and route of delivery are determined. Additionally, a schedule of immunization is created, which includes the age of first administration, number of doses, sequence of or interval between doses, duration of immunity, and potential need for booster immunizations.
This phase can take up to two years to complete and can cost anywhere from $9 million to $15 million.
Success rate: 50% of vaccines make it thorough this phase, which is only 2.2% of vaccines that begin the clinical trial process.

Phase III
Phase III is considered a long-term trial to catch any unexpected or rare events and to evaluate efficacy longer than the shorter, smaller phases. This phase recruits many more volunteers, in the thousands. The goal of this phase is to evaluate the vaccine in the most diverse population available, that should represent the population who will receive the vaccine. In this phase, the following questions are answered.
Is this vaccine still safe? The safety of the vaccine continues to be assessed and potential side effects or rare events are identified. This is performed for a longer duration in a more homogeneous target population.
This phase often lasts one to four years can cost anywhere from $40 million to $100+ million.
Success rate: At this point, 100% of vaccines make it thorough this phase, resulting in only 2.2% of vaccines that begin the clinical trials process seeking regulatory approval.
Is this vaccine effective? The clinical efficacy of the vaccine is determined, usually by monitoring the relative disease incidence among the vaccinated versus unvaccinated portion of the target population.

Regulatory Approval
After making it through phases I to III, the vaccine manufacturer must go before the regulatory authorities (often the FDA) for final approval before taking it to the market. During this review, the regulatory authorities review all data collected during all phases, specifically seeking evidence of safety and efficacy.
One in ten vaccines are not approved by the regulatory authorities and must continue data collection, or possibly scrap the entire vaccine study.
This phase takes an average of 1.32 years to complete and costs approximately $1.5 million to $2 million.
Success rate: 92% of vaccines make it thorough this phase, which is a only 2% of all vaccines that start the clinical trial process.

Phase IV
After a vaccine receives approval from regulatory authorities and is widely used in the general public, it enters what is referred to as phase IV. This is not a formal clinical trial phase that we are accustomed to, rather a continuous long-term assessment of the vaccine's efficacy and safety throughout the entire population. This continued assessment confirms that the clinical efficacy and safety determined during the clinical trials transfers to the general public.
At any time during this observation, if there are any concerns that are raised, the regulatory authorities may pause or completely stop the use of the vaccine until further investigation is completed to determine the impact of the concern.
COVID-19 Vaccine Timeline
